title: Add Multi-Digit Whole Numbers
description: Compute the sum of two multi-digit whole numbers using standard or place value strategies.
condition: Expression of the form a + b where a and b are multi-digit whole numbers.
response: Calculate and state the correct sum.
label: Production / Skill
notes: Algorithmic computation skill.

title: Choose Appropriate Rounding Level for Estimation
description: Determine whether rounding to the nearest ten or hundred gives a more reasonable estimate for a subtraction problem.
condition: A subtraction problem where estimation is requested (e.g., 326 − 278).
response: Select the rounding place (tens or hundreds) that gives a reasonable estimate and justify the choice.
label: Principle
example: For $326 - 278$, round to tens: $326 \approx 330$, $278 \approx 280$, so $330 - 280 = 50$. Rounding to hundreds gives $300 - 300 = 0$, which is not reasonable.

title: Construct Numbers that Round to Same Hundred
description: Form two three-digit numbers from given digits so that both round to the same hundred.
condition: Given a set of digits and requirement to form two three-digit numbers that round to the same hundred, using each digit at most once.
response: Select and arrange digits into two valid three-digit numbers whose rounded values (nearest hundred) are equal.
label: Principle / Model
example: Using digits 1,2,3,4,5,6: $156$ and $234$ both round to $200$.
notes: Requires integrating place value and rounding rules. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Divide Total into Equal Groups
description: Find the weight of one item by dividing the total weight by the number of equal items.
condition: Given a total weight and the number of equal groups/items.
response: Divide the total weight by the number of groups to find the weight of one group.
label: Principle / Model
example: If 3 blocks weigh 30 g total, then 1 block weighs $30 \div 3 = 10$ g.

title: Estimate Difference by Rounding to Tens
description: Round both numbers to the nearest ten to produce a reasonable estimate of a difference.
condition: Estimating the difference of two three-digit numbers where rounding to hundreds would produce an unreasonable estimate.
response: Round each number to the nearest ten and subtract the rounded values.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Includes reasoning about reasonableness of estimate.

title: Evaluate Subtraction Expression
description: Compute the value of a subtraction expression.
condition: A subtraction expression of the form a − b.
response: Calculate and state the difference.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Includes regrouping across place values when required.

title: Find Total from Two Quantities
description: Add two related quantities to find a combined total.
condition: Two quantities representing parts of a whole are known.
response: Add the two quantities to find the total.
label: Principle / Model
example: If postcards $= 307$ and stamps $= 416$, then total $= 416 + 307 = 723$.
notes: Typically follows determining a missing part in word problems.

title: Identify Range That Rounds to a Given Ten
description: Determine the interval of whole numbers that round to a specified multiple of ten.
condition: Given a target multiple of ten (e.g., 370) and asked which whole numbers round to it to the nearest ten.
response: State that numbers from 5 below the target ten to 4 above it (e.g., 365–374) round to that ten; identify boundary values as needed.
label: Principle / Model
example: Numbers that round to $370$ are $365 \le n \le 374$. The greatest whole number is $374$.
notes: Explains why 5 rounds up to the next ten.

title: Interpret 'More Than' Relationship
description: Translate a comparison statement using 'more than' into an addition or subtraction equation.
condition: A word problem states that one quantity is k more than another and gives one of the quantities.
response: Set up an equation representing the relationship (e.g., larger = smaller + k or smaller = larger − k).
label: Principle / Model
example: If stamps are 109 more than postcards and there are 416 stamps, then postcards $= 416 - 109$.
notes: Requires identifying which quantity is larger.

title: Read Analog Scale Measurement
description: Interpret the position of a pointer on a labeled analog scale to determine weight.
condition: Given an analog scale with labeled units and a pointer indicating a value.
response: State the numerical value and unit indicated by the pointer.
label: Production / Skill
example: If the pointer is at 9 on a kilogram scale, the weight is 9 kg.
notes: Interpreting tick marks may involve counting intervals.

title: Read Liquid Volume from Scale
description: Determine the starting amount of liquid by reading a labeled vertical scale.
condition: Given a container diagram with labeled liter markings and a visible liquid level.
response: State the number of liters indicated by the liquid level.
label: Production / Skill
example: If the liquid level aligns with 20 L, then the starting amount is 20 liters.

title: Read Temperature from Thermometer
description: Determine the temperature shown by interpreting the scale and fluid level on a thermometer.
condition: A thermometer image with labeled major marks and evenly spaced minor tick marks.
response: Identify the nearest labeled marks and count minor intervals to state the exact temperature.
label: Production / Skill
example: If the fluid is 6 small ticks above $70$ and each tick is 1 degree, the temperature is $76^\circ$.
notes: Visual measurement skill.

title: Read Value from Data Table
description: Locate and state the numerical value associated with a category in a table.
condition: Given a table with categories and corresponding numerical values.
response: State the number listed for the specified category.
label: Production / Skill
example: From the table, Panda corresponds to $45$ students.
notes: Basic data retrieval skill.

title: Read Value from Number Line
description: Determine the numerical value represented by a plotted point on a labeled number line.
condition: A point is plotted between labeled benchmark numbers with equal intervals.
response: Identify the exact value by counting interval marks from the nearest labeled benchmark.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a point is two small ticks past $550$ and each tick represents 10, the value is $570$.
notes: Requires interpreting equal spacing on number lines.

title: Represent Data on Bar Graph with Scale
description: Create a bar graph that accurately represents categorical data and choose an appropriate scale.
condition: Given categorical data with numerical values.
response: Select a consistent scale and draw bars with heights matching each value.
label: Principle / Model
example: For values 35, 25, 30, 45, use a scale of 5 and draw bars up to each value.
notes: Requires proportional reasoning about scale.

title: Round to Nearest Hundred
description: Round a three-digit number to the nearest hundred based on the tens digit.
condition: A three-digit number to be rounded to the nearest hundred.
response: If tens digit is 5 or more, increase hundreds digit by 1 and set tens and ones to 0; otherwise keep hundreds digit and set tens and ones to 0.
label: Principle / Model
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Round to Nearest Ten
description: Round a number to the nearest ten based on the ones digit.
condition: A number to be rounded to the nearest ten.
response: If ones digit is 5 or more, increase tens digit by 1 and set ones to 0; otherwise keep tens digit and set ones to 0.
label: Principle / Model
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Select Appropriate Weight Unit
description: Choose grams or kilograms based on the typical weight of an object.
condition: Given a familiar object and a numerical weight value with unit options (grams or kilograms).
response: Select the unit (grams or kilograms) that makes the statement reasonable.
label: Concept
example: A cat weighs about 5 kilograms (not grams).
notes: Requires real-world knowledge of typical object weights.

title: Select Reasonable Measurement Estimate
description: Choose the most reasonable measurement value for a real-world object based on typical benchmarks.
condition: A real-world object and multiple numerical answer choices with the same unit.
response: Select the value that is most reasonable given typical sizes or weights.
label: Concept
example: A notebook weighs about $300$ grams (not $30$ grams).
notes: Relies on benchmark knowledge. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Solve for Missing Value in Equation
description: Determine the number that makes an equation with a single operation true.
condition: An equation with one unknown and a single addition or subtraction expression (e.g., ___ = a − b or ___ = a + b).
response: Compute the expression and state the value that makes the equation true.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Focus is on evaluating the expression to determine the unknown.

title: Subtract Multi-Digit Whole Numbers
description: Compute the difference of two multi-digit whole numbers using standard or place value strategies.
condition: Expression of the form a − b where a and b are multi-digit whole numbers.
response: Calculate and state the correct difference.
label: Production / Skill
notes: Algorithmic computation skill.

title: Translate 'More Than' Statement to Subtraction
description: Interpret a comparison statement indicating how much more one quantity is than another and represent it with subtraction.
condition: A statement of the form 'A is X more than B' and the value of A is known.
response: Set up and compute B = A − X.
label: Principle
example: If a bear weighs $147$ kg more than a kangaroo and the bear weighs $204$ kg, then kangaroo $= 204 - 147 = 57$ kg.

title: Use Compensation Strategy for Addition
description: Adjust one addend to a friendly number and compensate by subtracting or adding the adjustment.
condition: Expression a + b where one addend can be adjusted to a nearby friendly number (e.g., 690 to 700).
response: Rewrite as a + (b ± k) ∓ k and compute equivalent sum.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Based on maintaining equality by inverse operations.

title: Use Compensation Strategy for Subtraction
description: Adjust both numbers in a subtraction problem by the same amount to create friendly numbers while preserving the difference.
condition: Expression a − b where b is close to a friendly number (e.g., 399 close to 400).
response: Rewrite as (a ± k) − (b ± k) and compute equivalent difference.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Uses property that adding/subtracting same amount to both terms preserves difference.
